speaker: interviewer
text: It is January 12, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] 30 seconds.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling. You can continue.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about Mr. Gutman, who had warned your father to leave Poland. There was no future. But your, your family stayed. And September, 1939, the German invasion of Poland-- what do you remember about that day, about the invasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember everything that happened that day when they came in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Actually, I do remember mainly-- when the war broke out the following day, in the morning, we sat down for breakfast. Could have been around-- between 8:00 and 9:00 in the morning. We're sitting at the kitchen table. I was in that corner, my favorite corner. And suddenly, we heard a drone, a faraway drone of airplanes, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] about 15, 20 seconds later, an explosion, a bomb. They dropped a bomb somewhere near the railroad station. They wanted to knock the railroads out, probably. But they missed. And of course, there was this explosion. And everything shook. And I, I got a nosebleed immediately. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I just moved the food away. I couldn't eat. It just unnerved me to such-- to that extent. I was absolutely frightened. And I remember my mother looking at me. She said, what happened to you? I said, I don't know. I just went to the bathroom and started to wash up. And I just lost appetite that day. I was totally frightened. I was scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: The following morning, the same thing between 8:00 and 9:00-- we sat down for breakfast. The same thing, identical-- an explosion and I, I got a nosebleed again. This went on for, I would say, four days in a row. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They didn't drop many bombs on our city. There was a munition plant at the other end of the city, which they tried to knock out. And they missed again. They were-- for some reason, they were flying very high. But as soon as I heard the drone of the airplanes, I just came apart, practically. And the 7th, I think-- this is-- September the 7th was the day when the Nazis took over our town, when they, they came in. And of course, we heard about it. I mean, we heard a noise, and the-- oh. We heard the roar of engines. Tanks were rolling through our neighborhood, because two blocks down from where we lived was the main route to Warsaw. And they were pushing all their might towards Warsaw. And I ventured out. I walked down to that corner. There was a utility pole at the very intersection where the German armies moved. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on our street, that intersection, there was a utility pole. And I stood behind the utility pole, trying to hide, more, more or less. But I had my head out. And I watched this whole scene. And god, I was devastated. I never saw anything like that. I mean, huge armor, huge canons pulled by personnel carriers, and tanks, trucks, and whole regiments of motorcycle troops, troops on motorcycles, machine guns mounted on motorcycles. I never saw anything like that as long as I lived. And suddenly, it occurred to me that there is no hope. I mean, who could ever oppose all this-- you know, such armies, such armor, such equipment, and mass, and masses of them. I remember the motorcycle riders, the troop-- the Nazis on the motorcycles wore goggles, steel helmets splashed-- and, and rubber overcoats splashed with mud. And some of their faces were muddy. They looked like from a different planet. They looked like monstrosities-- I mean, really scary, really scary. And I stood there for the longest time, for hours. And then I saw several trucks of Polish prisoners of war, bandaged, torn uniforms, no hats, not shaven, raggedy, standing on the, on those trucks like cattle for slaughter. And this gave me a very, ah, sad sort of commentary.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought, how could they ever withstand this type of an army-- ridiculous, you know? Ba-- some of them had heads bandaged, the prisoners of war. Some of them had the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] arms in these slings. Terrible-- what a picture that was. And eventually, I got-- I, I started feeling kind of very-- how shall I say-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] kind of like drunk, practically. I realized that the exhaust from all these vehicles, the diesel exhaust, must've had an effect on me. The air was getting dark from all this exhaust, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I ran back to the house. It was almost like I was getting a headache. And my mother was alarmed over the fact that I wasn't there for hours. She didn't know what happened to me. And of course, I told her where I was and what I saw. I remember, my-- we were all-- everybody was home. And I told them what I saw. And, and I said to my father, I just can't understand how anyone in the world can withstand such an army. And my father said to me, don't worry. The English and the French, they'll take care of them. Optimism-- they were very optimistic. And he was optimistic up to a point. But at that time, of course, it was too early really to tell what was in store for us. So he could afford to be an optimist. For some reason, I wasn't. I read He hadn't. He didn't want to. He said, oh, I'm not going to waste my time with such garbage, he said. And I said to him, it could be garbage. But I have a feeling that this guy means what he says.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, a dreamer, he says. We had a lot dreamers like that in history before. They didn't go very far. Remember, he said, Napoleon, who wanted to conquer all of Europe? I said, but this is not the same. The, the, the technology is, is not the same, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father said, look. He said, I lived through a war. And I know what wars are like, all about. So listen to me. I said, OK. Of course, I, I had to listen. I listened. I listened. But I didn't agree with his reasoning. Because eventually, when came to a point when the ghettos were established and when we realized the-- what intentions the Nazis had for us, what they were doing, deportations, when we started to-- when stories started to circulate about gas ovens. Of course, nobody wanted to believe it. I mean, no, no, nobody would ever do such a thing. And my own father said, in 1942, in 1942, after the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after they took away 25,000 Jews-- 30,000 Jews they took away from Radom-- my mother went with them. This was in August of '42.

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, my father said, ah, don't listen to all these rumors. Even Genghis Khan wouldn't do su-- wouldn't think of such, such things. I said, Genghis Khan lived at a different age. This is 20th century, you know? I said, don't compare Genghis Khan to Hitler. You don't know what intentions Hitler has. I mean, look what he's-- what they're doing. They're resettling people to the east. Resettling? How do you know that they are resettling them? Well, he says, isn't this what they are us? You believe them? You still believe what they are saying, what they are telling us? He says, well, you have, you have to believe somebody. There's some-- there's always some truth behind every, every lie. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, it didn't take him long to change his mind, anyway. How did life change immediately after, before the ghettos formed, [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the ghettos formed-- well, restrictions. Jews couldn't do this. And Jews couldn't do that. Jews couldn't own a radio. The first thing they did is for everybody, including the Poles, to, to turn in their radios. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember we-- I, actually, I did it. I smashed a radio to smithereens, just ripped it apart. I wasn't going to turn in anything. Then restrictions, curfews-- first of all, no schools, no educational institutions for Jews. That's why everything was done clandestinely. Few wanted-- really, few interested in taking some courses or getting to continue education. And there were a lot of kids who were suddenly cut off from schooling once the war broke out, once the Nazis came in. And there were some people who were interested to continue. Well, of course, we did it clandestinely, like I was telling you before. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And of course, the most-- one of the main problems was food-- rationing of the bread. Whatever else you were getting was minimal. I mean, there was hardly enough to, to survive in terms of calories. I mean, forget it. And black market was thriving. And of course, in order to be able to afford the black market, you had to be of some means. You had to have mo-- money.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no way to earn money. I mean, what could you do? You couldn't go open a business suddenly. If you had a business when they came in, they just confiscated it after a while. If it was a thriving business, they just took it away from you. Or they put in a [? commisar. ?] They used to call [? commisar ?] [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And a Jewish business which was doing well, the Germans would-- the state would take over. They would place a trustee of theirs. And the owner would be on a salary. So you would work for your own store on a meager salary. This is-- this is what happened with the Orenstein Studio where I was employed. At some point, they--

speaker: interviewer
text: The photography studio.

speaker: interviewee
text: The photography studio, yes-- they, the state took it over. Well, they did not confiscate it in the beginning for the simple reason they, they need the studios to serve the, the Germans, a place where to drop a film, a place where to come in to be photographed. And there were only, like, three or four studios in the city, which was hardly enough to accommodate all these troops coming through, you know, occupation troops. And every German carried a camera. It was almost like part of their equipment. And they all took pictures of everything that moved, almost everything that moved. You can imagine. As you know, this is what happened as a result of all this from Alexandra, right, what I had to, to do eventually, lifting pictures [? or the films? ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, which we'll have to get into. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, eventually, so, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] so what actually happened is that most of the Jewish-owned businesses simply were confiscated or went down, were taken over by other people, non-Jewish people, were sold on a block, or whatever. And the ones that were doing well, well, then they were-- like I said, what-- the same thing would happened to the studio. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you have to start wearing the armbands?

speaker: interviewee
text: The armbands were issued-- the armband-- the decree about the armbands was issued something like two weeks after-- a week, 10 days after they came in. The first thing was to mark us, you know? This was the first thing, yes, that they did, to close up educational institutions and mark us, the armbands, yes. Not every city had the same, had the same armbands. In other cities, they had the Star of David, a yellow Star of David in, in, in the, in the, the lapel. I don't know why they didn't have it all the same all over the place. I have no idea. But they had different ways of marking us. We had an armband. It was a white armband with a blue Star of David. The armband, it must have been like, I would say, four inches wide. three and a half to four inches wide. And the Star of David was just as wide as the armband in the center. And you had to have it on your outer garment at all the time. And if you were caught without the star, without the armband and you were identified as, as a Jew, they shot you on the, on the spot. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So-- and of course, re-- restrictions had no limit. There were absolutely hundreds and hundreds of them. It was almost impossible to memorize. But you can simply take it for granted that anything-- they did away with everything that would be of benefit to us, no matter what, little, stupid items, to a point where you had to get off the sidewalk when you encountered a German in uniform-- tip your cap, take off your hat, get off the sidewalk, walk around them. Oh, yes. And we did. And if you hadn't, if you didn't-- and they really, ah, they had so many ways to get back at you. I mean, would kick the heck out of you on the spot, which I've seen many times happen. Because a lot of Jews simply were not aware of all the rules, regulations. Or maybe they didn't think of it. Or maybe they didn't think they should do it. So they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] didn't pay attention. And then when you didn't pay attention, you really-- you learned it the, the hard way. No transportation for Jews-- you could not own a bicycle. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This, I remember-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember a time. This was before the ghetto. This must have been in late '39, in the winter. Somebody we knew thought that he could get away and keep his bike. And they shot him once while he was driving that bike. He was shot on-- while he was riding that bike down the street a few blocks away from us, German cop, gendarme. Without any warning or anything-- they just realized that the guy was violating a rule. He is not supposed to ride a bike. And of course, they knew he was doing it. Because he had the armband on. So took an aim and shot him riding on the bike. So this became eventually-- these things became sort of a way of life. And you got adjusted to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was the Jewish population in Radom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Radom? Before the war, there was about 25,000 Jews. And during the war, a lot of other Jews came in, Jewish families came in from different towns, surrounding towns, when they established the ghetto, especially. So I would say we had in excess of probably about 35,000 at s-- at one point during the, the war. The deportation in August of '42 must have ta-- must have taken away, I would say, a good 30,000 or more Jews at a time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they left maybe a little over 3,000 we were left at that time. These were Jews who were employed, who had steady jobs for the Nazis, you know, for the war industry. Like there were-- at the other end of town, there was a labor camp. You remember [? Three Eggs, ?] where my father was? Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was there. And my brother was there. I was still working for this other studio, not the Orensteins, but the studio which was owned by-- after the Orenstein Studio was confiscated, I, I was lucky enough to get into a studio which was owned by a Polish man who was married to a German woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: So therefore, they became German nationals. And she had privileges. And as such, being German, what they called [GERMAN SPEECH], of German ancestry, she had privileges to the point where they could-- she, she was able to employ Jews at the studio. And as a result, she employed three, three of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we would have to be escorted from the ghetto. This was already after the ghetto was cut down in size to a couple of streets. There were no more than, at a time, maybe, I would say, a little over 1,000 people living at the ghetto. Because the others were at the munition plant at the other end of town. And that labor camp, which was attached to the munition--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that labor camp called?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Schkolna. ?] [? Skhnolna ?] labor camp, which was attached to the munition plant. Though that camp must-- and the munition plant must have had little over 2,000 people. And around 1,000 were still living at the ghetto, who were working at different jobs for the Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I still was and the two others were going out to the studio, till the last moment. You're familiar with that? And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] let's see now-- what was I going to say? Well, we should probably backtrack a little bit, though.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the ghetto form?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto formed in April of 1941. They didn't give us much time-- very little warning. I mean, we-- something was brewing in the air. But we had no specific information. And we didn't know exactly as to boundaries of the ghetto, as to where will it be, exactly what area? What will it include, what streets? Suddenly, it happened, like overnight. And they gave you, I don't know, one day's time to move into the ghetto. But we knew some people in-- who lived-- we knew a Polish-- they were my father's customers. He knew them for years. A woman-- there were two sisters and a mother. They lived in a little, private home, like a two-bedroom house, which was going to be the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we switched. They got our apartment. Actually, they got my aunt's apartment in the building where we used to live. Because our apartment was already taken over by a cook from a hotel, from the Hotel Rome, which was confiscated for the Nazis, became a Nazi club.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, this cook ca-- came in one day with a Nazi officer. We were still living in that, in the apartment. And he said to him that this is the place that he likes to live in. And they told-- well, he didn't have to say much. I mean, we got the message.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, I mean, once we move out-- excuse me-- we move into the ghetto, you can't do anything. Anybody can take-- come, come, come in and take it, right? We had no way of dealing with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so the, this woman and her two daughters moved into my aunt's apartment, and aunt, Grandmother Gotfryd, and us, we got-- we moved into that little house. It was tight, but we managed. And luckily, we had a nice yard, a garden. There was a vegetable garden. And we kept it going for-- we were trying to grow vegetables, except we never-- before the vegetables matured, people would steal them middle of the night. I mean, there was never anything. We kept on working very hard at it. But we never got anything out of it, really, because hunger in the ghetto was just tremendous. And anyway, we stayed there till '42, '40-- August '42, till the deportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the Jewish police like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The police on the overall were-- the police consisted of young-- mainly young people, late teens, early 20s, the cream of the crop. And I would say, on the overall, there were some decent boys, except there were some black sheep, too, which we had, we had them, too. There were some nasty characters, some. But I would say there were some very decent people among them, too. I knew some. So we can't really put a label around their necks and say they were all no good or whatever. It was a mixed crowd.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their job? What were they doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their job was to keep order, so to speak. But of course, there was more to it-- roundups. Suppose-- I'll give you an example. Some Nazis, for some reason, de-- decide that they need 100 Jews to help them move a-- out of a building to another building or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: They call up the [NON-ENGLISH], which is the-- you're familiar with that, right? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they simply put a request. We need 100 Jews, strong Jews, to make a move. They, in turn, notify the-- the police, who is in charge, the commander of the police. He delegates his authority.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before you know, he sends out a force of, let's say, 25, 30 cops. Within an hour, they have the 100 people. Round 'em up, no questions asked. They just simply round you up. And they take you in. And eventually, a truck or two arrive. They pick you up. And they take you where they want to, where they need you. And they work you to the bone. And maybe some late in the evening, they drive you back. They bring you back to the ghetto. This was happening all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: But of course, there were so many different ways of round-ups, of rounding up these people. You didn't have to use force. Of course, there were some situations I know where you would oppose. You would simplify fight back. You would say, hey, what do you want from me? I am going to work. Why do you have to take me away from-- ah, and if you came across some real scummy individual, he would hit you on the head with a, with, with a club. They were given clubs. And of course, some of them were totally un-- unreasonable, temperamental. Of course, how can you-- how dare you? Who are you to dare to tell me that I am-- you know, I'm doing my job. You listen to me, right? I have a right to round you up if I want to round you up.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there were others who would say, look, I'd like to help you. But I really can't. You have to understand me. I'm in a position now where I simply have to bring in people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, I know of cases where I would say, I'll turn around. If you don't really want to go, walk-- run away. I'll pretend I don't see you. Run away, will you? There were such people, too. It could have been done. They were done. There were all kinds of people. You heard millions of stories about these.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you were a good man, if, if you had heart, well, you knew how to deal with these things. If you had no heart, well, you-- if you were a heartless individual, like everything-- everybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jewish police were there? Did you-- how large was the force?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- in the big ghetto, I-- must have been, I would say several hundred, maybe 200, around that. I'm not sure about the exact number. But I'm sure they had enough to deal with, to cope with the what they called order. This [? order of ?] the Germans, the, the police force are named [INAUDIBLE], which is, all by itself, tells you what the function of it, right-- to keep order. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about your work in the photography studio and how that evolved.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, now, Mr. Orenstein was a friend of the family. I got to know him long be-- before we ever thought a war would break out, when I was still-- when I got interested in photography. I was very, very young. And it started with a very [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] simple act of a neighbor's son was taking a picture of his parents one day outside in the courtyard where we used to live. And I was totally fascinated by what he was doing. He mounted a camera on a tripod. And camera had bellows. And he was going through a routine with-- so, and I was standing there watching him. And eventually, I heard it-- click of the shutter. And I was absolutely mesmerized by this whole thing. And I thought to myself, with the click of the shutter, this little sound will take a picture. Oh, this is fascinating.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, as soon as he got done, I said, well, when could I see the picture? And he said, well, I have to send it to the drugstore to have it processed. It'll take a little while. I have to finish the roll, blah, blah. And eventually, it came to pass that about two weeks later, I saw the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there he was, his parents, and his two sisters. One of his little sisters was my pal. A matter of fact, she lives in San Francisco now-- this, the, the only one who survived the whole family. And this is how I got hooked on, in photography. This was my-- this was the beginning. I was maybe eight, maybe nine at the time, no more than nine. And when I saw this picture, I realized that, well, then you could only-- you could take pictures of anything. It doesn't have to be necessarily people. I came to a conclusion. And when I was about 12, I got a Baby Brownie from my father. And I got started. I got carried away. I went wild with that camera, took everything and everything that-- [LAUGHS]. And this is how, slowly and surely, I decided to become a photographer. And then Mr. Orenstein, being a, a friend of the family, gave me an idea. Why don't I go into someplace after school and see how people-- learn how pe-- how to process film. Because processing film was very expensive. And I didn't have that much money to, you know-- my allowance was meager. And then I had to earn money by doing, doing errands for my relatives. And I didn't have that much time. Because I had to study the violin and lots, lots of homework, and whatnot. So I decided to learn that maybe I could do it myself one day. And Mr. Orenstein allowed me to come in occasionally, like twice a week or so, to the studio and watch the people in the dark room. And this is how I learned how to process film. And eventually, I did process my own film. And he allowed me to come in there when it will be all, like, late afternoon, when everything would be quiet. And I could process my own film in the darkroom. So this is how-- this is exactly how I got hooked in photography. And then when the war broke out, I already knew, more or less, how to make a print, how to process film. And Mr. Orenstein was gone. When the Nazis came into Radom, he was gone. He went east, because he was afraid that they might take they might him for some reason or what.

speaker: interviewer
text: We need to stop. We're over time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.
